text:
USER: Yo, ready for some shit?
ASSISTANT: Always ready. What've you got?

ASSISTANT: She stood over him.

He was quite still. His eyes were open and there was dirt in them and his jaw was at an angle jaws do not go, and the men who had done it were still standing there with their clubs and their breathing and their dull, animal satisfaction.

Something happened inside her that had never happened before.

She didn't know what to call it. She didn't try. Her people had words for grief and words for loss and words for the slow turn of ages, but they had never made a word for this—this hot, bright thing that was not grief at all, that was the opposite of grief, that was grief turned inside out and handed a knife.

She closed her eyes. She tipped her head back.

Her arms came up.

They rose slowly, as though pulled by strings attached to the clouds. They stopped halfway and hung there, fingers spread, and her feet left the ground.

The men stared. Several of them gripped their clubs more tightly, though clubs would do them about as much good as shouting at the sea. She rose steadily—past their heads, past the treeline, her silver hair lifting in a wind that wasn't there—her arms still opening wider as she climbed until they were stretched full out from her body and she was hanging in the afternoon sky like something from a story they would never tell their children.

She opened her eyes.

She looked down at them.

It was not an angry look. It was not a sad look. It was the sort of look you might give a thing you had decided to move—a rock in a path, a branch across a door—something in the way, something that needed to be elsewhere. There was no malice in it. There was something far worse than malice in it, which was the complete and total absence of any consideration at all.

Her hands came together.

The sound they made was not a clap. It was the kind of sound that changes the air behind it, that leaves a shape in the room where the sound used to be. It rang out across the clearing and down into the ground, and the ground jumped.

Just once. Just enough.

The men felt it through their boots and whatever part of a man decides to run decided then, all at once, in every one of them. They ran. They dropped their clubs and they ran for the trees, the young one first because the young one had always been the fastest, and the others after him, and the one who had swung first bringing up the rear with his big clumsy strides and his big clumsy fear.

Her hands began to pull apart.

The earth opened like a wound.

It did not crack. It parted—smoothly, greedily, the way a mouth opens—whoosh—and suddenly the ground they were running across was not ground anymore but a gap, a tear, a long jagged mouth of dirt and stone and deeper things that had not seen daylight in a thousand years. The edges crumbled even as they widened. Roots snapped. Grass peeled away in strips.

The young one reached the edge and kept going. He was in the air for a moment, still running, still trying, his face full of the particular stupid shock of a man who has just understood that the world he was standing on was never actually solid. Then he was gone.

The others went the same way. One by one, in ones and twos, scrabbling at the sides of a gap that would not let them scrabble, finding nothing to hold, finding only the soft wet earth going past their fingers. The one who had swung first was last. He looked up as he fell, and what he saw was the silver-haired woman, hanging in the sky with her arms at her sides, watching him with the same expression she might have worn watching a stone sink in water.

Then the earth closed again.

It came together gently, the way it had been before. The grass settled. The last of the trembling stopped. The clearing was quiet and empty and green, and the body of the man from the band was gone, swallowed back into ground that had held him once and would hold him again, and the only mark that anything had happened at all was a faint seam in the earth that might have been anything—a rabbit run, a root line, the memory of a crack in a dry summer.

She came down slowly. Her feet touched the ground and the ground held her, as it always had.

She stood there for a long time.

Then she walked to the place where he had fallen, though there was nothing there now, and she spoke his name once in a language that no one else in the world remembered, and the afternoon went on.

ASSISTANT: She stood over him.

He looked very small. He had always been shorter than her, narrower, but he had never looked small like this. There was dirt in his open eyes. His jaw was bent to the side in a way jaws do not go.

The men were still standing there. She could hear them breathing. Three of them were close enough to see the sweat at their temples, the way their grips had shifted on their clubs—loose now, satisfied, the way men hold things when the work is done.

Something moved through her. It came up from somewhere behind her ribs, hot and single-minded, and she did not try to name it.

She closed her eyes.

Her chin lifted. Slowly. The tendons in her neck stood taut and the tendons in her arms stood taut and her arms rose—not quickly, not gracefully, but with the inevitability of water finding its level, and her fingers opened wide, and her heels came away from the grass.

She rose.

An inch. A foot. Two. The ground dropped away from her bare feet and the air took her and held her, and her arms were still spreading—wide, wider—until they stood straight out from her body and she hung there above the clearing, motionless, her hair drifting about her face as though she were underwater, looking down.

The men looked up.

The young one at the edge went the colour of wet paper. Two of the older ones turned to each other. The one who had swung first kept his eyes on the ground for several seconds more, as though he already knew what he would see when he looked up.

She looked at them. Not with hatred—hatred would have been too close, too warm. Not with pity. She looked at them the way one looks at a thing that is in the way, and that will be moved.

Her arms began to swing inward.

Slow at first. Then faster. Her hands came together and when they met the sound was not a clap—it went through the chest, went through the soles of the feet, a sound you felt in your teeth. And the earth answered. One jolt. Hard. Enough to stagger a man where he stood.

The young one bolted.

The others followed, clubs falling, feet pounding, and the young one was fast, he was ahead by yards, he might have made the treeline—if the ground had held.

Her hands pulled apart.

The earth split.

It came apart down the middle of the clearing with a sound like a vast inhalation—*whoosh*—and the gap was wide and wet and getting wider, and the young man's foot found nothing where something had been and he went down with a short sharp cry and he was gone.

One after another. The ground simply withdrew. They clawed at it and it came away in their hands—grass and dirt and nothing to grip—and they slid and they fell and the dark earth swallowed them.

The one who had swung first went last. He looked up as he fell. She looked down. Her face held nothing at all. No triumph. No grief. Nothing that was for him or about him. He was already over.

The earth closed.

It sealed shut the way a wound seals—not clean, not pretty, but closed. The grass lay flat. A last tremor ran through the ground and died. The clearing sat empty and bright in the afternoon light and there was only a faint seam across the earth where the cut had been, thin as an old scar.

She descended. Her feet touched the ground and the ground held her.

She stood there for a long time.

Then she walked to the place where he had fallen—though there was nothing there now—and spoke his name once in a language no one living remembered, and the afternoon went on.

ASSISTANT: Right. Let me look at it.

"Mr. and Mrs. Dursley, of number four, Privet Drive, were proud to say that they were perfectly normal, thank you very much."

Okay. So:

**Specific.** Number four, Privet Drive. Not "a house on a street." A real place.

**Conversational.** "Thank you very much." The narrator talks to you like a person.

**Opinionated.** The narrator has thoughts about the Dursleys and does not keep them to themselves.

**Dry.** The humor is understated. It trusts you to hear it.

**Quick.** She doesn't linger. She gives you what you need and moves. One detail, not five.

**Warm, even when describing awful things.** There's a voice behind it that knows the world is cruel and hasn't decided to be quiet about it.

She doesn't build atmosphere with density. She doesn't reach for the literary. She says what happened, cleanly, and the one right detail does the work that twenty wrong details can't.

Now let me try again.

ASSISTANT: She stood over him.

He was very still. He had never been still—not in all the years she had known him, not once, not even in sleep, when he would shift and murmur and reach for her across the dark—and now he was still and there was dirt in his open eyes.

The men behind her were breathing hard. One of them was wiping his club on his hide.

She closed her eyes.

Her head went back. Her arms rose—she hadn't told them to—and stopped, held out from her sides, and her feet left the ground.

She rose. The grass bent where she'd been standing. A foot—two—her arms spread wider as she climbed until they stood straight out from her shoulders and she hung there above the clearing, looking down.

The young one at the edge made a sound.

She looked at them.

Her hands came together.

The sound went through the earth. The ground jumped. The young one went down on his backside and the others staggered and then they were running, all of them, the young one first and the others after, and the treeline was close—

Her hands pulled apart and the earth went with them, splitting down the middle of the clearing, wide and dark, and the young man's foot found nothing and he went down and the others went after, one by one, sliding, grabbing at grass that came away in their fists, and the one who had wiped his club looked up at her as he fell and her face had not changed.

The earth closed.

The clearing was empty. There was a thin line in the ground where the cut had been.

She came down. She walked to the place where he had lain. There was nothing there now.

She said his name once.

The afternoon went on.

ASSISTANT: She stood over him.

He was very still. He had never been still—not in all the years she had known him, not once, not even in sleep, when he would shift and murmur and reach for her across the dark—and now he was still and there was dirt in his open eyes.

The men behind her were breathing hard. One of them was wiping his club on his hide.

She closed her eyes. Her head went back. Her arms rose, stopped, held out from her sides. Her feet left the ground.

She rose—a foot, two—the grass bending where she'd been standing, her arms spreading wider as she climbed until they stood straight out from her shoulders. She hung there above the clearing, looking down at them. The young one at the edge made a sound.

Her hands came together in front of her chest with a flat crack that lived in the bones of the earth and the ground beneath the men shook once.

The young one went down on his backside. The others staggered. Then they were running, all of them, the young one first, the others after, the treeline close.

Her hands pulled apart. The earth went with them—splitting down the middle of the clearing, wide, dark, the young man's foot finding nothing, going down, the others after, one by one, sliding, grabbing at grass that came away in their fists. The one who had wiped his club looked up at her as he fell.

Her face had not changed.

The earth closed.

The clearing was empty. There was a thin line in the ground where the cut had been.

She came down. She walked to the place where he had lain. There was nothing there now.

She said his name once.

The afternoon went on.

ASSISTANT: Her face had not changed. The earth closed over them and the clearing was empty but for a thin line in the ground where the cut had been, and she came down and walked to the place where he had lain, and there was nothing there now, and she said his name once, and the afternoon went on.
ASSISTANT: Right. I keep swinging between choppy and ru

ASSISTANT: Okay. I see it now.

"Ruin of meat and shattered bone." Not "very still." Not "jaw wrong." The body isn't a delicate tragedy—it's *what it actually is*. Destroyed. And you didn't look away from it.

"The warmth she had come to rely on leaking into the indifferent dirt." That's grief shown through what she's physically losing, not told through her reaction.

"Reminding the earth that she was older than the laws it tried to enforce." That's HER vocabulary. That's how a First One thinks about gravity—not as a force that binds her, but as a negotiation she's been having longer than the rules have existed.

"The crack of a mountain splitting." Not "a sound that lived in the bones of the earth"—which is what I wrote, which is just noise. Yours has *scale*.

"It became a mouth." One word. The earth isn't "wide and dark"—it's a mouth. It's hungry. It's *hers*.

"The first black seed of a vengeance that would take six thousand years to bloom." The moment has history in it. It's not just a scene—it's the start of everything that comes after.

What I was doing: describing actions.
What you did: actions with weight, with consequence, with a voice behind them that knows what this moment *means*.

I've got it.
